title: The Trump Administration’s Threat to Scientific Research
excerpt: In The Nationalization of American Science I warned that the Trump administration’s rewriting of the seemingly mundane Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance was a tremendous threat to America’s historically successful decentralized system of science funding. Many others are now sounding the alarm. It’s not surprising that organizations like the AAAS oppose the rule, albeit with unusually […]
content_html:
In The Nationalization of American Science I warned that the Trump administration’s rewriting of the seemingly mundane Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance was a tremendous threat to America’s historically successful decentralized system of science funding. Many others are now sounding the alarm.

It’s not surprising that organizations like the AAAS oppose the rule, albeit with unusually strongly worded dissents:

This latest move is a brazen power grab by the Director of the Office of Management and Budget to buck the will of Congress and the American people and will make future discoveries less likely. If this rule becomes final, Americans’ hopes for future cures, national security and economic strength will rely on the scientific sensibilities of the nation’s chief bureaucrat. Alzheimer’s disease will not be cured by a budget analyst from either political party.

But we are now seeing strong pushback from independent thinkers such as:

Grayson Logue writing at The Dispatch:

A sweeping new rule proposed by the Trump administration could remake how that money is awarded and give the president and his political appointees discretion to cancel funding or target recipients for virtually any reason—with little opportunity for recourse.

White House officials argue the new rule is necessary to assert more accountability over federal grantmaking, but observers fear the shift will expand opportunities for politicization, abuse, and even corruption for an administration that has already demonstrated a penchant for using the levers of the federal government to punish partisan enemies and reward ideological allies. 

Dan Drezner:

if I was trying to ruin American leadership in scientific research this is pretty much the kind of rule I would write…One of the genuine difficulties with observing the second Trump term is that the assault on state capacity and impartiality has been so multipronged that it is difficult to keep track of everything going on. But these proposed rule changes are monumental and catastrophic.

and Noah Smith:

MAGA’s attack on science is even worse than it looks…despite science’s overwhelming popularity and public trust, Trump and his administration are launching an unprecedented and devastating attack on American science — cutting funding, and forcing science projects to undergo ideological review by government commissars.

It may be that the Trump administration has pushed too far, but my real worry is that we are losing an equilibrium. Science was never completely independent of politics, of course, but even at the worst of times, funding was decentralized and the culture-war material that dominated the headlines was never more than a tiny fraction of the whole. Like an independent judiciary, independent science has been an American virtue. COVID policy, gender policy, and now the Trump administration’s weaponization of these mistakes may have destroyed that equilibrium.

As I wrote in my original post, we are adopting the loser policies of authoritarian nations but those policies are the norm elsewhere for a reason. Centralized control of science is the default because it serves the people in power of whatever party. Decentralization is the fragile exception—a historically unusual achievement that is easier to destroy than rebuild.

Addendum: And here is Andrew Gelman.

blocks.html_en:

sentences.en: A sweeping new rule proposed by the Trump administration could remake how that money is awarded and give the president and his political appointees discretion to cancel funding or target recipients for virtually any reason—with little opportunity for recourse.
sentences.zh: 特朗普政府提出的一项全面的新规则可能会重新制定这笔资金的发放方式，并赋予总统和他的政治任命者自由裁量权，可以出于任何原因取消资金或瞄准接受者，而几乎没有追索的机会。

sentences.en: White House officials argue the new rule is necessary to assert more accountability over federal grantmaking, but observers fear the shift will expand opportunities for politicization, abuse, and even corruption for an administration that has already demonstrated a penchant for using the levers of the federal government to punish partisan enemies and reward ideological allies.
sentences.zh: 白宫官员认为，新规则对于对联邦拨款实行更多问责是必要的，但观察人士担心，这一转变将扩大政府政治化、滥用权力甚至腐败的机会，因为政府已经表现出倾向于利用联邦政府的杠杆来惩罚党派敌人和奖励意识形态盟友。

sentences.zh: 但这些拟议的规则变更是具有里程碑意义和灾难性的。

sentences.zh: 也许特朗普政府做得太过分了，但我真正担心的是我们正在失去平衡。

sentences.en: Science was never completely independent of politics, of course, but even at the worst of times, funding was decentralized and the culture-war material that dominated the headlines was never more than a tiny fraction of the whole.
sentences.zh: 当然，科学从来都不是完全独立于政治的，但即使在最糟糕的时期，资金也是分散的，占据头条新闻的文化战争材料也只不过是整体的一小部分。

sentences.en: Like an independent judiciary, independent science has been an American virtue.
sentences.zh: 就像独立的司法机构一样，独立的科学一直是美国的美德。

sentences.en: COVID policy, gender policy, and now the Trump administration’s weaponization of these mistakes may have destroyed that equilibrium.

Decentralization is the fragile exception—a historically unusual achievement that is easier to destroy than rebuild.

sentences.en: As I wrote in my original post, we are adopting the loser policies of authoritarian nations but those policies are the norm elsewhere for a reason.
sentences.zh: 正如我在原来的帖子中所写，我们正在采用威权国家的失败者政策，但这些政策在其他地方却是常态，这是有原因的。

sentences.en: Centralized control of science is the default because it serves the people in power of whatever party.
sentences.zh: 对科学的集中控制是默认的，因为它为任何政党的当权者服务。

sentences.en: Decentralization is the fragile exception—a historically unusual achievement that is easier to destroy than rebuild.
sentences.zh: 权力下放是一个脆弱的例外——这是一项历史上不寻常的成就，摧毁比重建更容易。

sentences.en: Addendum: And here is Andrew Gelman.
sentences.zh: 附录：这是安德鲁·格尔曼。

content_html_zh:
在《美国科学的国有化》中，我警告说，特朗普政府重写看似平凡的联邦财政援助条例，对美国历史上成功的分散式科学资助体系构成了巨大威胁。 许多其他人现在也敲响了警钟。

毫不奇怪，像美国科学促进会这样的组织反对这一规则，尽管他们的异议措辞异常强烈：

这一最新举措是管理和预算办公室主任公然夺取权力，违背国会和美国人民的意愿，并将使未来发现的可能性变得更小。 如果这一规则成为最终决定，美国人对未来治疗方法、国家安全和经济实力的希望将依赖于国家首席官僚的科学敏感性。 任何一个政党的预算分析师都无法治愈阿尔茨海默病。

但我们现在看到独立思想家的强烈反对，例如：

格雷森·洛格在《电讯报》上写道：

特朗普政府提出的一项全面的新规则可能会重新制定这笔资金的发放方式，并赋予总统和他的政治任命者自由裁量权，可以出于任何原因取消资金或瞄准接受者，而几乎没有追索的机会。 白宫官员认为，新规则对于对联邦拨款实行更多问责是必要的，但观察人士担心，这一转变将扩大政府政治化、滥用权力甚至腐败的机会，因为政府已经表现出倾向于利用联邦政府的杠杆来惩罚党派敌人和奖励意识形态盟友。

丹·德雷兹纳：

如果我试图破坏美国在科学研究方面的领导地位，这几乎就是我会写的规则……遵守特朗普第二任期的真正困难之一是，对国家能力和公正性的攻击是如此多方面，以至于很难跟踪正在发生的一切。 但这些拟议的规则变更是具有里程碑意义和灾难性的。

和诺亚·史密斯：

MAGA 对科学的攻击比看起来更糟糕……尽管科学具有压倒性的受欢迎程度和公众信任，特朗普和他的政府正在对美国科学发起前所未有的毁灭性攻击——削减资金，并迫使科学项目接受政府委员的意识形态审查。

也许特朗普政府做得太过分了，但我真正担心的是我们正在失去平衡。 当然，科学从来都不是完全独立于政治的，但即使在最糟糕的时期，资金也是分散的，占据头条新闻的文化战争材料也只不过是整体的一小部分。 就像独立的司法机构一样，独立的科学一直是美国的美德。 新冠疫情政策、性别政策，以及现在特朗普政府将这些错误武器化，可能已经破坏了这种平衡。

正如我在原来的帖子中所写，我们正在采用威权国家的失败者政策，但这些政策在其他地方却是常态，这是有原因的。 对科学的集中控制是默认的，因为它为任何政党的当权者服务。 权力下放是一个脆弱的例外——这是一项历史上不寻常的成就，摧毁比重建更容易。

附录：这是安德鲁·格尔曼。
